segments.s034-g17: همه (subject) + در هوای (prepositional phrase) + فریدون (object of longing) + بدند (past copula). در هوای is an idiom meaning "in the mood/desire for"; بدند is the archaic past plural of بودن (they were) and here functions like "they were/they felt" — together: "all were longing for Fereydun." Word order is Topic — prepositional phrase — object — verb (typical classical Persian).
segments.s034-g18: که introduces the reason here (= "because"). از marks origin/ cause ("from/because of"). درد ضحاک is a genitive: "the affliction/pain of Zahhak." پرخون is an adjective meaning "blood‑filled/bloody." بدند again is the past plural "they were." Together the clause gives the reason: "because they were bloodied by Zahhak's affliction."
segments.s034-g19: ز دیوارها = "from the walls." خشت is the accusative noun "bricks/mudbricks." وز here is a poetic fusion of و + ز (= "and from"), linking the next source (the roofs); treat it as the coordinating particle "and (from)." Word order: source preposition + place, then item thrown.
segments.s034-g20: به + کوی اندرون = "into/onto the alleys/inner quarter(s)." تیغ و تیر و خدنگ is a simple list "sword and arrow and dagger." به marks direction/toward; اندرون specifies "inside/inner quarters." The three weapons are coordinated by و (and).
segments.s034-g21: ببارید is an impersonal/past verb "it rained/fell." چون = "like/as." ژاله is a simile noun "dew (or drops)." ز ابر سیاه = "from a black cloud." The clause uses a poetic simile: things fell "like dew" from a dark cloud. Word order: verb — comparison — source phrase.
segments.s034-g22: پئی = "a foot/step." را is the definite/object marker emphasizing پئی. نبد is an archaic negative existence verb (classical form corresponding to نبود) meaning "there was not / did not remain." بر زمین جایگاه = "a place on the ground." Together: "there was no place on the ground for a foot." Note the classical negative n‑form and SOV feel.
segments.s034-g23: به شهر اندرون = "into/within the city." هر که = "whoever / every one who." برنا is an adjective meaning "young / vigorous." بدند is the past plural "they were." The clause says that every young person was within the city’s inner quarters. Word order: direction/place — quantifier + relative — adjective — verb.
segments.s034-g24: چه here introduces the second group and works like "and/or even" ("and likewise"). پیران = "elders." که = relative "who." در جنگ = "in war/battle." دانا = adjective "wise/experienced." بدند = past plural "they were." Together: "and likewise the elders who were wise in war."
segments.s034-g137: گسسته شد is the past participle گسسته ('broken/separated') plus the auxiliary شد ('became'), giving the sense 'became separated' or 'was torn away'. از خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from his own (self/nature)'; here خوی + the enclitic ش (his) produce the reflexive sense 'his own/self'. The coordination و پیوند او links a second noun phrase: پیوند ('bond, tie') followed by او (the possessive/third-person pronoun) indicating 'his bond' — note the archaic/poetic omission of an explicit ezāfe (پیوندِ او) is normal in classical Persian verse.
segments.s034-g138: بمانده is the perfect/passive participle meaning 'remaining' or 'was left' and functions as the main predicate of this clause. بدان گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in that manner/so' — here written as ب + دان + گونه in the token list, but in classical Persian بدان گونه (bdān gūne) is a fixed phrase 'in that way'. در بند او is a prepositional phrase 'in his fetter/bonds' (در in + بند fetter/bond + او his) modifying بمانده; the final word-order places this locative/condition after the predicate as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s034-g33: This is a coordinated noun phrase: سپاهی (troops/soldiers) + و + شهری where شهری is used as a substantive 'townsfolk / city-dwellers'. به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/form of' (به + کردار) modifying the preceding nouns; کوه is the simile 'mountain'. Word order is noun(s) + simile phrase (typical Persian), so the group is described as 'like a mountain'.
segments.s034-g34: سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'entire/throughout' qualifying the following clause. به جنگ اندر آمد is a compact verb phrase meaning 'entered into battle' — به + جنگ (into the battle) + اندر (classical preposition 'in/into') + آمد (past tense 'came'). گروه is the subject/summary 'the company/troop' that performs آمد. The verb آمد is simple past.
segments.s034-g35: از آن شهر روشن is a locative phrase 'from that bright city' (از = 'from', آن = 'that/the'). یکی تیره گرد is a noun phrase 'a certain dark گرد'; here تیره 'dark' qualifies گرد which, in this context, functions as 'a cloud/whirl of dust' (classical/poetic usage). The sequence yields 'from that bright city there rose a dark cloud/dust.'
segments.s034-g36: برآمد is the simple past 'arose/issued forth'. که introduces the result clause 'that/so that', followed by خورشید شد لاجورد where شد is the past of شدن 'became' used as a copular/result verb and لاجورد describes the resulting color/state of the sun. The clause means 'so that the sun became (lapis) blue.'
segments.s034-g37: پس آنگاه are temporal adverbs 'then/afterwards' introducing the action. ضحاک is the subject (proper name). شد + چاره‌جوی: شد is past 'became' used as a copula and چاره‌جوی is an agentive noun/adjective 'seeker of a remedy' (چاره + -جو), so 'he became / was a seeker of a remedy' or 'he sought a remedy.'
segments.s034-g38: ز لشکر is a classical prepositional phrase 'from/with the army' (ز = 'from'). سوی کاخ is 'toward the palace' (سوی = toward, کاخ = palace). بنهاد روی is a dated/poetic verbal expression: بنهاد (past of بنهادن 'to set/place') + روی 'face' — together 'he set/laid his face toward' i.e. 'he turned toward / directed his face toward.' The whole segment means he turned from/with the army toward the palace.
segments.s034-g39: The phrase literalmente reads 'به آهن سراسر بپوشید تن' = 'he clothed the body entirely in iron.' Here به + noun (به آهن) marks the material/instrument ('in/with iron'), سراسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/throughout' modifying the action, and بپوشید is the literary verb form (ب- prefixed to پوشید) used as a 3rd-person past/perfective 'he put on / he clad'. The object تن ('body') follows the verb for emphasis, a flexible word order common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s034-g40: بدان is a fused adverbial ('ب' + element) functioning here as 'by that/thereby' (literary), and تا introduces the purpose/result clause meaning 'so that.' نداند is a 3rd-person negative present/subjunctive/jussive form of دانستن ('may not know' / 'would not know'), making the clause purposive. کسش is 'his person/anyone' used under negation to mean 'no one,' and ز انجمن means 'from/among the assembly' with ز used in its poetic sense 'from/among.'
segments.s034-g89: فریدون is the subject (proper name) followed by the epithet فرزانه in apposition ('the wise Fereydun'). بنواختشان is a past-tense verb (third-person singular) with the enclitic object pronoun -شان 'them'. In this epic context نواختن is poetic/archaic and here means 'set/appointed' rather than the literal 'play/strike'; the verb carries the object already in -شان.
segments.s034-g90: بر اندازه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'according to measure/rank'; بر پایگه is another locative phrase 'on (their) bases/posts'. ساختشان is a past verb ('made/established') with the same enclitic object -شان 'them'. The two verbs share the same object and together express appointment/installation according to rank.
segments.s034-g91: همی is an iterative/habitual particle used in classical Persian (here marking repeated or customary action). پندشان داد = 'gave them advice' (داد is past 'gave', -شان object). و کرد آفرین uses کرد as a light verb 'did/made' with آفرین as the object/complement meaning an acclamation or praise—'and he uttered/poured forth praise.'
segments.s034-g92: Again همی marks habitual action. یاد کرد is a compound verbal expression 'remembered' or 'spoke of' (کرد is past 'did'). از introduces the object/topic of یاد کردن (i.e. 'of/about'), so از جهان‌آفرین = 'of the World‑Creator' (he recalled/mentioned the Creator).
segments.s034-g93: همی گفت is a formulaic past/habitual phrase 'he would say' or 'he constantly said'. کاین is a contracted classical particle = 'که این' introducing reported speech ('that this...'). جایگاه من است is a nominal predicate 'this seat/position is mine' with the possessor من placed before the copula است.
segments.s034-g94: به here has an instrumental/causal force 'by/with'; نیک اختر is a fronted adjective+noun ('auspicious star'). بومتان is بوم 'homeland/land' with the 2nd‑person plural possessive -تان 'your'. روشن است is the predicate 'is bright/illuminated' — overall 'by an auspicious star your land is bright.'
segments.s034-g95: که here is a subordinating/relative particle 'that/for'. یزدان پاک is the subject with پاک following the noun as an adjective ('the pure God'). از میان گروه is a prepositional phrase 'from among the group/host' (از میان = 'from among').
segments.s034-g96: برانگیخت is a past transitive verb 'raised/roused' (3rd singular). ما را is the object 'us' with را marking definite/objective emphasis. ز is the poetic variant of از 'from', and البرز کوه places the mountain name before کوه in classical word order ('Mount Alborz').
segments.s034-g81: شما دیر مانید و خرم بوید is two coordinated predicates about the addressees. شما (you, 2nd‑person plural/formal) is the subject. دیر مانید uses دیر (an adverb meaning ‘long/for a long time’) with مانید (present/subjunctive/optative form of ماندن here meaning ‘to remain/linger’) to state that they have lingered; the conjunction و links the second clause خرم بوید, where خرم (joyful) is a predicate adjective and بوید is an archaic/copular verb-form (poetic form of بودن) meaning ‘is/are’ — this combination yields “are joyful.” The word order places the subject up front and predicates after, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s034-g82: به رامش سوی ورزش خود شوید is an admonitory/subjunctive clause giving the action to be taken. به رامش functions adverbially (“in/with peace” or “peacefully”), سوی (toward) marks direction, ورزش خود literally ‘your/one’s own exercise/practice’ is the destination, and شوید (plural subjunctive/imperative form of شدن) issues the exhortation ‘become/go’ or more loosely ‘go/return.’ The prepositional sequence (به ... سوی ...) frames the manner (peacefully) and direction (toward the training).
segments.s034-g83: شنیدند یکسر سخنهای شاه is a simple past clause: شنیدند is the 3rd‑person plural past of شنیدن (‘they heard’). یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb (here ‘at once/straightaway’ or ‘entirely’), and سخنهای شاه is the direct object ‘the king’s words/speeches’ (سخنهای is the plural/object form of سخن, modified by شاه). Word order is verb–adverb–object here, acceptable in verse.
segments.s034-g84: از آن مرد پرهیز با دستگاه is a prepositional/nominal phrase giving the content or topic of what was heard. از آن مرد = ‘concerning/of that man’ (از marking source/about), پرهیز is a noun meaning ‘caution/forbearance,’ and با دستگاه (‘with the court/establishment’) further qualifies the manner or the sphere — دستگاه here means the royal court/establishment. Together they mean ‘about that man’s caution with the court.’
segments.s034-g85: وز آن پس همه نامداران شهر opens the temporal/local frame. The first token appears as وز but functions syntactically like از (classical/poetic variant) meaning ‘from/and’ or may be a scribal/orthographic variant; آن پس = ‘after that,’ and همه نامداران شهر = ‘all the renowned men of the city’ (subject noun phrase). The phrase gives who is involved after the prior event.
segments.s034-g86: کسی کش بد از تاج وز گنج بهر is terse/elliptical and uses compact classical diction. کسی is ‘someone/anyone’ (here functionally ‘no one’ in negative context), کش can be an agentive element or verbal root (classically used to form compounds or mean ‘drawing/taking’), بد = ‘bad/evil,’ and از تاج وز گنج بهر literally ‘from the crown and from the treasure for (their) advantage.’ The line uses condensed word order common in epic verse; exact syntactic relations are idiomatic—likely saying that none of the city’s worthies sought evil from crown or treasure (i.e., they did not covet them).
segments.s034-g87: برفتند با رامش و خواسته is a past‑tense clause: برفتند (they departed/they went away) is 3rd‑person plural past. با رامش و خواسته is a circumstantial adverbial phrase formed with با + two nouns (‘with tranquility and desire/wish’) describing the manner and accompanying feeling of their departure.
segments.s034-g88: همه دل به فرمانش آراسته is a verbless/resultative clause typical in Persian: همه دل (‘all hearts’) is the subject, به فرمانش (‘to his command’ or ‘by his decree’) is a prepositional complement with the possessive suffix -ش (‘his’), and آراسته is a past‑participle/adjectival predicate meaning ‘adorned’ or ‘made ready/arranged’ — here idiomatically ‘hearts were disposed in obedience to his command.’ Word order is subject + prepositional complement + predicate adjective.
segments.s034-g9: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بی‌راه here functions adverbially/adjectivally meaning 'wrongly/unjustly'. مر is an archaic clitic for 'me/my' (classical form of مرا), so ز بی‌راه مر = 'from me unjustly'. کاخ را is the direct object with را marking definiteness/emphasis, and بام و در lists the parts of the palace ('roof and door') that were taken.
segments.s034-g10: گرفت is simple past 'he took/seized'. The conjunction و links the two actions. به functions as a preposition 'out of/for' with کین 'vengeance, spite' (به کین = 'out of spite/vengeance'). اندر is the locative/particle 'in/within' and آورد is past 'brought'; سر is the direct object 'head'. The whole clause reads 'and, out of vengeance, he brought (in) the head.'
segments.s034-g11: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. سپاهِ فریدون is the subject 'Fereydun's army' (noun + name). آگه شدند is a compound past meaning 'became aware/learned' (آگه 'aware' + شدند 'they became'), so the clause = 'When Fereydun's army learned (of it)'.
segments.s034-g12: همه 'everyone/all' is the subject; سوی آن راه = 'toward that road' (سوی = toward, آن = that, راه = road/path). بی‌ره شده‌اند uses the adjective/phrase بی‌ره (variant of بی‌راه) with شدند 'they became' to indicate they acted without restraint or properly 'heedlessly/irregularly' — here it describes the manner of their moving toward the road.
segments.s034-g13: ز is the preposition 'from' or 'with' in this context showing accompaniment. اسپان 'horses' (plural) modified by جنگی 'warlike/for war' = 'war-horses' or 'cavalry'. فرو is an adverbial prefix 'down(ward)' and ریختند is past 'they poured/flowed', so فرو ریختند = 'they poured down/rushed forth (on their horses)'.
segments.s034-g14: در آن جای تنگی = 'in that narrow place' (در = in, آن = that, جای = place, تنگی = narrowness). برآویختند is a past verb meaning 'they leapt upon' or 'sprang on/attacked', so the clause describes them springing into the confined spot to attack.
segments.s034-g15: همه lists quantity 'every/all': بام و در مردمِ شهر = 'the roofs and doors of the townspeople' (مردمِ شهر = townspeople). بود is the past copula 'was/were' linking to the following clause; this segment sets the location/context where people stood (on roofs and at doors).
segments.s034-g16: کسی 'someone' is the existential/indefinite subject. کش here is the agentive/active formative (as in compounds meaning 'fighter/striker'), so کسی کش = 'someone a fighter' or simply 'someone ready to fight'. ز جنگاوری = 'from/with valor' (ز = from/of, جنگاوری = martial skill/valor). بهر بود uses بهر 'for/ready' plus بود 'was' to mean 'was available/ready for battle' — together: 'there was someone, a fighter, ready because of his valor.'
segments.s034-g57: This is a small nominal clause: 'بیآمد' here functions as a past negative ('did not come') and precedes the subject. 'سروش خجسته دمان' is a title-like phrase (soroush = messenger/angel; خجسته = auspicious/blessed; دمان = hem/skirt) with adjectives following the proper name in the classical word order.
segments.s034-g58: This contains direct speech compressed into narrative form. 'مزن' is an imperative negative ('do not strike') addressing an action; 'گفت' stands in for 'he said' (classical past narrative marker); 'کاو را نیامد زمان' is the quoted content meaning 'for his time has not come' — here 'نیامد' = 'did not/has not come' and 'را' is the object/topic marker attached to 'کاو' (anaphoric 'that one / him').
segments.s034-g59: 'همیدون' is a proper name (subject). 'شکسته' is an adjective/participle 'broken' modifying it. 'ببندش' is an imperative verb 'bind him/close him' formed from the imperative stem plus the enclitic object -ش (him/it). 'چو' is the comparative 'like/as' introducing the simile 'چو سنگ' ('like a stone').
segments.s034-g60: 'ببر' here is the imperative 'take/carry' (not the animal). 'تا' introduces a temporal limit meaning 'until'. 'دو کوه' is the measure 'two mountains' (direct object/goal), and 'آیدت' is a present/future-form verb 'come to you' with the enclitic object/indirect pronoun -ت. 'پیش تنگ' is a tight adverbial phrase 'before you (presents) narrow/close' — 'پیش' = before/presence, 'تنگ' = narrow/close.
segments.s034-g61: The line uses inverted classical order. 'به کوه اندرون' = 'into/within the mountain' ('به' = into/towards, 'اندرون' = inside). The copula 'بود' ('was') follows the locative phrase, and 'بند او' ('his fetter') is the logical subject placed after the verb for emphasis (literally: 'Into the mountain's interior was his fetter').
segments.s034-g62: This clause negates the idea of any severing: 'نیاید' = 'will not come/does not come' (negated verb), and its object is 'برش' ('a cutting' / 'a severing'). 'خویش' + enclitic 'ش' = 'his disposition/own' (here meaning 'his nature'), and 'و پیوند او' coordinates 'and his bond' — the whole means 'no cutting will befall his nature and his bond'.
segments.s034-g63: 'فریدون' is the subject. 'چو' here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'بشنید' is the past tense 'he heard'. 'نآسود' is a negative participle/adjective 'not at rest / unrestful' (ن- prefix + آسود), and 'دیر' functions adverbially 'long' — together 'he did not remain long at rest' (i.e., he quickly rose to action).
segments.s034-g64: 'کمندی' is the object 'a lasso/snare'. 'بیاراست' is a perfective/past action verb here meaning 'he made/arranged/trimmed' (classical use of آراستن = to prepare/ornament), so 'he fashioned/prepared a lasso'. 'از' = 'from/out of', 'چرم' = 'leather/skin', and 'شیر' = 'lion' (compound 'چرم شیر' = 'lion-skin').
segments.s034-g1: جهاندار ضحاک is a titular noun (title + name) meaning 'the ruler Zahhak' with the title preceding the name. از آن گفت‌گوی is a prepositional phrase where از introduces the source/reason ('because of/of that'), آن points back to 'that', and گفت‌گوی ('conversation') is the noun governed by از.
segments.s034-g2: به جوش آمد is an idiomatic inchoative: به + noun + آمد gives 'came into a state of X' (here 'came to a boil' = 'became furious'). بنهاد is an archaic past form of نهادن (to put/place/set) and with روی ('face') in the phrase زود بنهاد روی it means 'quickly turned his face' i.e. 'set out' or 'made a move'. و simply links the two actions.
segments.s034-g3: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). شب گردش روز is a poetic genitive/attributive group: 'the night, the circuit/turning of the day' (گردش governs روز). پرگار زد is a verbal phrase with زد (past of زدن) meaning 'struck/marked/drew' and پرگار ('compass') as the object or instrument in the metaphor.
segments.s034-g4: فروزنده را مهره is a somewhat inverted/poetic ordering meaning 'the radiant one's bead' (فروزنده = 'the radiant one'); را here highlights/marks the definite entity. در قار زد is a prepositional phrase with در 'in/into' and قار (a poetic/obscure word here) followed by زد 'struck', completing the action 'struck the bead into/against qar.'
segments.s034-g5: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('that/so that'). برنهادند is past plural of برنهادن (to put on / to place on / to lift up) and governs زین ('saddle'), so برنهادند زین = 'they put on/placed the saddle.'
segments.s034-g6: بر آن is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on those' (آن referring back to previously indicated animals/people). بادپایان باریک‌بین is a sequence of two adjectives/epithets ('fleet‑footed' and 'keen‑eyed') modifying the understood noun (horses/steeds or riders) that is omitted for brevity — a common poetic ellipsis.
segments.s034-g7: بیامد is a simple past verb 'came/appeared'. دمان functions here as the subject (in the poem it reads as a name or collective), followed by a comitative phrase با سپاهی گران ('with a heavy/weighty army'), where گران is a postpositive modifier qualifying سپاهی.
segments.s034-g8: همه + نره is 'all (the) manly/virile', and دیوان here (in Shahnameh usage) refers to divs/demons; جنگاوران is an appositive noun 'warriors' describing them. The phrase therefore lists the ranks that accompany the subject: 'all the manly divs, warriors.'
segments.s034-g113: بسا + روزگارا is a terse poetic noun phrase: بسا is an adverb/adjective meaning “many/often,” modifying روزگارا (here “times/ages”). که introduces a relative/nominal clause (“that/which”), and بر + کوه و دشت literally means “upon/over mountain and plain” (بر = on/upon; word order is preposition + noun; و simply links the two landscape nouns).
segments.s034-g114: گذشته‌ست is the perfect/past compound (گذشته + است) meaning “has passed/has gone.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بسیار here means “many/much” (not merely intensifier), followed by خواهد + گذشت forming a future sense: خواهد is the auxiliary ‘will’ and گذشت (here the verb stem “pass”) yields “will pass” — a prediction that many more times will pass.
segments.s034-g115: بر آن گونه is a fixed phrase “in that way/manner” (بر = “in/on,” آن گونه = “that manner”); ضحاک را uses the object marker را to mark the definite object (Zahhak). بسته here is an adjectival or participial form meaning “bound/tied,” and سخت is an adverb/adjective meaning “firmly/tightly,” describing how he was bound.
segments.s034-g116: سوی is a preposition meaning “toward(s).” شیرخوان is a compound noun (literally “lion-feast” — here the place where lions are fed). برد is here a past action verb ‘took/carried/led’ (classical past of بردن) meaning they conveyed him; بیداربخت is a descriptive epithet “ill‑fated / wretched.”
segments.s034-g117: همی is an archaic/poetic progressive/adverb meaning “continually / was” used with the verb. راند is past tense “drove” (from راندن) and او را is the object “him” (او + object marker را). به + کوه اندرون = “into the mountain interior” (به = into/towards, اندرون = inside). Word order is S(implicit) + همی + verb + object + prepositional phrase.
segments.s034-g118: همی خواست: همی again gives continuous/intentive coloring and خواست is the past of خواستن “wanted/desired.” کآرد is an archaic subjunctive/optative form meaning “that (it) may do / that (he) might bring about” (ک- is the relative/subjunctive marker fused to آرد). سرش را = “his head” with possessive ش and object marker را. نگون is an adjective meaning “overthrown / downcast”; together the clause expresses an intention to cause his downfall.
segments.s034-g119: بیآمد here functions as a past arrival verb (‘came/arrived’) in context (classical Persian usage), though the provided senses do not list that directly; هم = “also,” آن گه = “at that time/then,” and خجسته سروش is the appositive noun phrase “the auspicious Soroush” (Soroush = a divine messenger/angel).
segments.s034-g120: به خوبی can be read adverbially as “in a good/auspicious way” or simply linking manner; یکی راز = “one/a certain secret” (یکی = one/a certain), گفتش is the compound verb “(he) said to him/her” (گفت + ش = said to him), and به گوش literally “into the ear” (به = to, گوش = ear) — i.e. whispered into his ear.
segments.s034-g73: سپاهی is the noun 'soldier' (subject). نباید is an impersonal modal negative ('should not' / 'it ought not to be') followed by the conjunction که introducing the clause; با + پیشه‌ور indicates accompaniment ('with a craftsman'). Word order places the subject before the modal, and the clause begun by که will continue with the verb later.
segments.s034-g74: به یک روی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with one face / in the same manner' (به marking an adverbial relation); جویند is the present plural verb 'seek/pursue' (here dependent on نباید که, giving the sense 'should not seek'); هر دو هنر = 'both arts/skills' (هر used distributively to mark the two items together).
segments.s034-g75: یکی ... و یکی is a simple enumerative: 'one (is) ... and another (is) ...' with the copula omitted (a common classical Persian pattern). کارورز = 'worker/craftsman' and گرزدار = 'mace-bearer' (the second word names a military role).
segments.s034-g76: سزاوار here means 'appropriate/fit for'; the phrase سزاوار هر کس پدید است کار literally orders 'the fitting work of each person appears' — پدید (to appear) + است (is) gives the sense 'becomes apparent' and کار is the noun 'work/task'. The unusual word order (predicate before subject) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s034-g77: چو here functions as 'when' (introducing a conditional/temporal clause). The clause pairs demonstratives and the noun کار ('this work' / 'that work') around the verb جوید ('seeks/pursues'): 'when this work seeks that, that work [seeks] this' — a mirror construction emphasizing reciprocal, improper mixing of roles.
segments.s034-g78: پرآشوب is an adjective 'full of turmoil/tumultuous'; گردد (from گشتن) is a verb meaning 'becomes' (third-person present/subjunctive/formal), and سراسر زمین = 'throughout the land'. Together they form the result clause: 'the whole land becomes tumultuous.'
segments.s034-g79: به بند اندر است is a fixed poetic expression meaning 'is in bonds / is imprisoned' (به + بند + اندر = 'into fetters within'); آن‌که introduces a relative clause 'the one who...' and ناپاک بود uses the past form بود 'was' to describe that person's moral state ('was impure/corrupt').
segments.s034-g80: جهان را uses را to topicalize 'the world'; ز here means 'because of' (causal), کردار او = 'his deeds', و باک بود = 'there was fear' (باک 'fear/concern' with بود 'was'). The whole clause says that the world had fear on account of his actions.
segments.s034-g105: مهان is the plural subject 'the guests'. پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'in his presence/before him' placed after the subject. خاک دادند is a simple past verb phrase (دادند = 'they gave') with خاک ('the earth/dust') as its object; the following بوس is a noun 'a kiss' that indicates the manner/result — together the idiom means 'they gave a kiss to the ground', i.e. 'they kissed the ground' (an act of prostration/humility). Word order is typical of older Persian: subject + adverbial phrase + object + verb + result-noun.
segments.s034-g106: ز درگاه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the court/threshold' (ز = 'from'). برخاست is a past-tense verb 'rose/was raised' (3rd person). آوای کوس is the subject/nominative after the verb (inverted order): آوا + ی is 'the sound/voice of' and کوس is the genitive noun 'the drum' — together 'the sound of the drum'.
segments.s034-g107: دمادم is an adverb meaning 'constantly/continually'. برون‌رفت here functions as a verbal phrase 'issued forth/advanced' (past narrative). لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. ز شهر is the prepositional complement 'from the city'. The order (adverb + verb + subject + prepositional phrase) is typical of classical narrative style.
segments.s034-g108: وز here is the fused poetic sequence و + ز (i.e. 'and from'), introducing the next clause; آن شهر = 'that city'. نایافته is a past participle/adjectival form 'having not found / not found'. هیچ functions as an indefinite negator 'any/none' and بهر is the preposition 'for/with regard to'. Together the clause means roughly 'and from that city, having found none for that purpose…'.
segments.s034-g109: ببردند is a past transitive verb 'they took/led away' (3rd pl). ضحاک را is the definite direct object marked by را. The adjectives/participial modifiers بسته خوار follow the object and describe Zahhak's condition: بسته = 'bound/tied' (past participle) and خوار = 'despised/humiliated'. This order (verb + object + modifiers) is common in epic prose/poetry.
segments.s034-g110: به پشت هیونی is a prepositional phrase 'onto the back of a beast' (به = 'onto/to', پشت = 'back', هیونی = 'a beast/animal'). برافگنده is a past participle/verbal adjective 'having flung/placed/raised' describing what was done to him, and زار is an adjective 'wretched/woeful' qualifying his state. The sequence describes the manner of their carrying off Zahhak.
segments.s034-g111: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/kept on'. راند is the past verb 'drove/expelled'. از این‌گونه means 'from/of this sort' (i.e. 'in this manner/these creatures') and تا introduces the limit 'until/as far as'. شیرخوان is a compound 'lion's-feast' or 'lion's lair/feeding-place' — here it marks the extent/destination up to which the driving continued.
segments.s034-g112: جهان را is the topical/accusative-marked 'the world' (را marking specificity/topic). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. این 'this' is the demonstrative, and بشنوی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you hear' used in the temporal clause 'when you hear this'. پیر خوان is a vocative phrase 'old/aged reciter' (پیر = old, خوان = 'reciter' here), addressing the bard/listener after the clause.
segments.s034-g41: This is a prepositional/nominal phrase describing how she holds the snare. به + noun here indicates motion/treatment into or into the grip ('into/with'); چنگ 'clench/grasp' is the noun of grasp; اندرون 'inside/inner' specifies the inner/inside hand or inner place; شست‌یازی is a poetic compound describing action by the palm/hand (an archaic/poetic noun), and کمند is the head noun 'snare/lasso'. Word order is typical Persian: preposition + modifier(s) + head noun.
segments.s034-g42: برآمد is a past tense/preterite verb meaning 'rose/ascended'. It is followed by بر + بام کاخ بلند, where بر + noun = 'upon/on the ...' and بام کاخ بلند is a noun phrase 'the high palace roof' (بام 'roof', کاخ 'palace' as the noun modified by بلند 'high/lofty'). The verb applies to the subject implied in the previous segment (the woman or the snare).
segments.s034-g43: بدید is the simple past (3rd singular) 'he/she/it saw'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. سیه 'dark/black' is an epithet modifying نرگس, here used as a feminine epithet/name ('dark-eyed Narges'). شهرناز is an epithet/appellation 'city-proud' (poetic epithet). Word order is verb + object phrase (typical in older poetry where the verb can precede the object).
segments.s034-g44: پر از is the idiom 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of/from'). جادویی is an adjective/noun 'magical/enchanting' modifying the preceding noun phrase. با فریدون به راز is a prepositional phrase: با 'with' (accompanying), فریدون is the name (here the mythic hero), and به راز 'in secret' (به used adverbially to make 'راز' into 'secretly'). Together this gives 'full of enchantment, with Fereydun in secret.'
segments.s034-g45: دو is the numeral 'two' repeated for parallel description: 'دو رخساره روز' = 'two faces/cheeks like day' where رخساره 'face/cheek' is qualified by روز 'day' (a bright comparison). و links to 'دو زلفش' = 'two tresses/locks', with the possessive suffix ش attached to زلف (zulf‑sh = 'her locks'). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', and شب 'night' completes the simile. The line uses compact, poetic apposition and simile.
segments.s034-g46: گشاده is a predicate adjective 'open' (here 'was opened' or 'fell open'); the following prepositional phrase به نفرین ضحاک لب literally orders modifiers: به نفرین 'to/by the curse', ضحاک 'Zahhak' (proper name in genitive relation), and لب 'lip' is the thing affected. In poetic word order the adjective precedes the modifying phrase; the sense is 'her lip was opened by Zahhak's curse.'
segments.s034-g47: The clause begins with به مغز اندرش where به +'مغز'+'اندر' marks location 'into/within the brain/mind of her' (اندر + ش is archaic/poetic 'in her'). آتش 'fire' (figurative, 'passion') + رشک 'envy' is the subject phrase 'the fire of envy'. خاست is an archaic/simple-past verb 'arose' (classical Persian past). The whole reads 'in her mind the fire of envy arose.'
segments.s034-g48: This clause has the verb افگند (past of افکندن, 'threw/cast') with its object کمند 'snare' and target ایوان 'iwan/porch' expressed by به ایوان ... اندر (two locational markers; poetic redundancy for emphasis). راست here is an adverb 'straight/directly' (i.e. 'straightway' or 'directly'). Word order is flexible and poetic: locative + object + locative particle + verb + adverb.
segments.s034-g121: "که" introduces a subordinate clause ("that"/"which") linked to the previous speech; "این بسته را" is the definite object (را marks the object) meaning "this parcel/packet"; "تا دماوند کوه" uses تا in the sense "as far as/to" and places the destination after it in poetic inverted order (دماوند followed by کوه = "Mount Damavand").
segments.s034-g122: "ببر" is an imperative ('take!') with the understood 2nd-person subject; "همچنان" functions adverbially ('in that manner/thereby'); "تازیان بی‌گروه" is a noun phrase 'the Arabs without a group/companions' (بی‌گروه = 'without company'), so the clause says: take X to Damavand, thereby leaving the Arabs companionless.
segments.s034-g123: "مبر" is an archaic negative imperative ('do not take'); "جز کسی را" means 'except someone' (کسی as the exception, marked by را); the relative clause "که نگزیردت" follows: که introduces 'who' and نگزیردت is negative + verb with the enclitic -ت (you) meaning 'will not forsake/leave you' (classical present used for future/intent).
segments.s034-g124: "به هنگام سختی" is a temporal adjunct 'at the time of hardship'; "به بر گیردت" is a verb phrase where به + بر = 'into/on the arm/into the bosom' and گیردت ('گیرد' + enclitic -ت) means 'he will take you' or 'he will carry you' — classical present used for future sense, so together 'will take you into his arms when hardship comes.'
segments.s034-g125: "بیآورد" is a literary/narrative verb form of آوردن here meaning 'he brought' (classical narrative past); "ضحاک را" is the definite object (Zahhak); "چون نوند" is a simile 'like Navand' (چون = 'like/as' and نوند here functions as a proper name/rare term).
segments.s034-g126: "به کوه دماوند" marks the destination/place 'to Mount Damavand' (prepositional phrase); "کردش" is کرد + enclitic -ش 'he made/did to him' (i.e. 'he put him'); "به بند" is a prepositional phrase 'into fetters/in bonds' — together 'he made him into bonds' = 'he bound him.'
segments.s034-g127: "به کوه اندرون" is a locative phrase 'inside the mountain' (به + مكان + اندرون); "تنگ جایش" is 'a narrow place/its narrow spot' (تنگ modifies جای; the suffix ش = 'his/its'); "گزید" is the past of گزیدن 'he chose' — typical SOV/order: location and modifiers precede the verb گزید.
segments.s034-g128: "نگه کرد" here means 'kept watch/kept (it) guarded' (نگه as 'watch/keeping' + کرد); "غاری بنش" is a compact poetic phrase 'a cave-seat' (غار + بنش 'seat/place to sit'); "ناپدید" is an adjective 'unseen/hidden' describing the state — so the clause says he kept guard and made the cave-seat unseen/hidden.
segments.s034-g49: نه ... و نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. Here the phrase is elliptical: the verb ('to think of / to care for') is omitted and understood from the context, so 'نه از تخت یاد و نه جان ارجمند' means 'neither (did he) remember/think of the throne nor (did he) value his precious life.' 'از' links 'تخت' to 'یاد' with the sense 'of/concerning'.
segments.s034-g50: فرود آمد is a poetic compound meaning 'descended / came down' (فرود 'descent' + آمد 'came'). The following prepositional phrase 'از بام کاخ بلند' ('from the roof of the lofty palace') gives the source/place of that descent; adjective بلند follows the noun phrase to qualify the palace.
segments.s034-g51: به دست اندرش is a locative/possessive cluster meaning 'in/at his hand' (به دست = with/in the hand, اندر + ش = 'in him' / 'his'). آبگون modifies دشنه ('a bluish dagger'), and بود is the past copula, so the whole clause reads 'there was a bluish dagger in his hand.'
segments.s034-g52: The structure به خون ... تشنه بود uses به to mark the object/goal of desire ('thirsty for'), خون = 'blood', پریچهرگان = 'the fair‑faced (beauties)', and تشنه بود = 'was thirsty'. Together: 'he was thirsty for the blood of the fair‑faced ones.'
segments.s034-g53: ز بالا = 'from above' (ز is the poetic 'from'). چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'. پی بر زمین برنهاد is literal word order: پی 'the foot' + بر زمین 'onto the ground' + برنهاد (past) 'placed/put', i.e. 'when he set his foot on the ground.'
segments.s034-g54: فریدون is the subject; به کردار باد is a simile 'in the manner of / like the wind' (کردار = 'manner, conduct', باد = 'wind'). بیآمد here is used poetically to indicate his coming; in classical contexts this form can imply sudden or unexpected arrival — 'came' (rendered here as 'came' / 'arrived').
segments.s034-g55: بر آن = 'on that / at that (moment)'. The phrase گرزه گاوسر دست برد is best read as 'Gâusar put his hand on (seized) the mace' — گرزه 'mace', گاوسر a proper name, and دست برد (lit. 'laid hand') = 'seized' or 'took up'. Word order is topical 'on that' + object phrase + verb.
segments.s034-g56: بزد is the past tense 'he struck'. بر سرش = 'upon his head' (بر + سر + enclitic ش = 'his'). ترگ بشکست خرد is compact poetic phrasing: ترگ 'the rag' (or shred/turban), بشکست 'was broken' (past), خرد 'small' = 'was broken to small pieces.'
segments.s034-g129: بیآورد is a past narrative verb meaning ‘he brought/produced’ (classical Persian past). The direct object مسمارهای گران follows it: مسمارهای is the plural noun ‘nails’ with the plural suffix -های, and گران is a post‑nominal adjective ‘heavy/weighty’ describing the nails.
segments.s034-g130: به جایی is a prepositional phrase ‘into/to a place’ (به + جایی). که introduces a relative clause ‘where/that’, and مغزش نبود is a past negation: مغز + possessive -ش ‘his/its brain’ + نبود ‘was not’. اندرَان is an archaic locative adverb meaning ‘therein/among them’, placed at the end to mark location.
segments.s034-g131: فرو is an intensifying adverb ‘down/deeply’ attached to the verb; بست is the past tense ‘he bound/fastened’. دستش is the object ‘his hand’ (دست + enclitic -ش). بر آن کوه is the locative phrase ‘on/upon that mountain’, and باز here functions as an adverb ‘again’ (postposed).
segments.s034-g132: بدان is an adverbial/concatenated form meaning ‘with that / thereby’ linking to the purpose clause; تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ‘so that’, and بماند is the present-subjunctive/past-purpose form ‘it may remain’. به سختی is an adverbial phrase ‘with hardship / firmly’, and دراز qualifies the remaining state as ‘long’ (i.e. lasting).
segments.s034-g133: ببستش is the past verb ‘he bound/tied him’ (ببست + enclitic object -ش). بر آن گونه is a locative/phrase ‘in that manner’ (بر + demonstrative آن + گونه ‘way/manner’), and آویخته is a past participle/adjective ‘hung’, modifying گونه (postpositive adjective).
segments.s034-g134: وز here is an archaic connective meaning roughly ‘and/from’ (classical contraction of و از); او is the pronoun ‘he/it’; خون دل is a genitive phrase ‘blood of the heart’ (خون + دل), and بر زمین ریخته is a verbal phrase ‘has been poured/spilled upon the ground’ with ریخته as the perfective participial form.
segments.s034-g135: از او is an ablative ‘from him / of him’ indicating source or association. نام ضحاک is the noun phrase ‘the name (of) Zahhak’ (name followed by the proper name in apposition), and چون خاک شد is a predicative comparison ‘became like dust’ (چون = ‘like/as’, خاک = ‘dust’, شد = past ‘became’).
segments.s034-g136: جهان is the subject ‘the world’. از بد او is an ablative/causal phrase ‘from/because of his evil’ (از used causally here). همه is an intensifier ‘all/entirely’, and پاک شد is the passive/resultative ‘was purified/was made clean’ (شد = became; پاک = pure).
segments.s034-g25: سوی (toward) + noun phrase + شدند: classical Persian often places the directional preposition before the whole noun phrase. شدند is the third‑person plural past form of شدن; here it functions as an intransitive/resultative verb meaning 'they turned/moved' toward the army. The whole segment therefore reads 'they turned toward Afrīdūn's army.'
segments.s034-g26: ز introduces the origin/source ('from'); نیرنگ is an abstract noun 'stratagem/trick'; ضحاک is the possessor/agent of that stratagem; بیرون شدند is a compound of the adverb بیرون ('out, away') plus شدند ('they became/went'), here meaning 'they came out' or 'they emerged/escaped.' Together: 'they emerged from Zahhāk's trickery.'
segments.s034-g27: خروشی (a cry/clamour) is the noun subject placed before the verb; برآمد is the past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'; ز + آتشکده is a prepositional phrase 'from the fire‑temple.' Word order is subject → verb → source phrase, a common ordering in poetic Persian.
segments.s034-g28: که introduces the content of the cry (a transmitted speech/clause). بر تخت is a locative phrase 'upon the throne'; اگر is the conditional 'if'; شاه باشد uses باشد (subjunctive/present form of بودن) to express the hypothetical 'if there be/is a king'; دده is a predicate/noun 'elder' (here proposed as the fitting condition or status). The clause thus reports the fire‑temple's proclamation: 'If there is a king on the throne, (let him be) an elder.'
segments.s034-g29: همه 'all' introduces the scope; پیر و برناش is a paired phrase 'the old and the young' (برناش is the adjective for youth; the -ش here is a bound form in the manuscript, but the sense is 'the youthful'). فرمان is the object 'command/decree'; بریم is first‑person plural present/subjunctive (from بردن 'to carry/take') used here as 'we will carry out/execute.' The segment therefore issues a general injunction: 'All, old and young, carry out the command.'
segments.s034-g30: یکایک is an emphatic adverb 'one by one / each and every'; ز گفتار او is a prepositional phrase 'from his speech/words' (ز = from); نگذریم is first‑person plural negative form of گذشتن 'to pass/let pass' and here means 'we will not pass over/omit' — i.e. 'we will not neglect a single one of his words.' Word order places the adverb first, then the source phrase, then the verb.
segments.s034-g31: نخواهیم is first‑person plural negative of خواستن 'we do not/will not want' and here expresses refusal ('we will not'); بر گاه is a locative/abstract compound meaning 'at the court/place (of rule)' (بر + گاه); ضحاک را names the addressee/target with the object marker را, marking definiteness/emphasis. Grammatically this segment states the refusal target: 'We will not [go to / submit at] Zahhāk's court.'
segments.s034-g32: مر is the dative/ethical pronoun 'to/for me' or an emphatic clitic used here to heighten personal rejection; آن is the demonstrative 'that'; اژدهادوش is a compound adjective 'dragon‑shouldered' (a stock epithet); ناپاک is an adjective 'impure/unclean'; the final را again marks the whole phrase as definite/emphatic. Together the segment supplies a pointed appositive description: '— that dragon‑shouldered, impure one.'
segments.s034-g97: بدان is the injunction 'know' composed of the prefix ب (here the verbal prefix be-) + دان 'know' and introduces the clause. تا is the subordinating particle 'that/so that' linking the following clause. جهان از بد اژدها is a noun phrase with از 'from' marking the source (the world freed from) and بد modifying اژدها as 'the evil of the dragon'. Word order in poetry allows the predicate introducer (بدان تا) to precede the rest.
segments.s034-g98: به فرمان گرز من is an agent/instrument phrase 'by the command of my mace' (گرز من = 'my mace', possessor follows the noun). آید is the present/future-meaning verb 'comes/will come' and is followed by رها used predicatively as 'free' or 'released' (classical word order places the adjectival/predicative element after the verb: 'comes free' = 'will be freed').
segments.s034-g99: چو here means 'when'. بخشایش آورد is a verb phrase where آورد (3rd sg. past) 'brought/granted' takes بخشایش 'forgiveness' as its object. نیکی‌دهش ('one who gives goodness' / 'the benefactor') is the actor; in poetic word order the subject follows the verb.
segments.s034-g100: به نیکی is an adverbial/benefactive phrase 'to/with goodness' (i.e. in a kindly manner or into goodness). بباید is the impersonal/modal 'one must/it is necessary' and takes the following verbal noun/infinitive سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' as its complement; رهش 'his release' is the object of سپردن (the possessive is implicit from the preceding benefactor).
segments.s034-g101: منم is 'I am' (first-person copula form). کدخدای جهان means 'the headman (leader) of the world' with the possessor following the noun, and the adverbial phrase سر به سر ('throughout/entirely') emphasizes totality — here it intensifies 'of the whole world'.
segments.s034-g102: نشاید is an impersonal negative 'it is not fit/it should not be' and takes the verbal noun/infinitive نشستن 'to sit/stay' as its subject/complement. به یک جای بر is a locative phrase 'in/at one place' (بر is the older prepositional element 'on/upon'), so the whole clause means 'it is not fitting to sit in one place.'
segments.s034-g103: وگر نه 'but if not' introduces a counterfactual condition. ایدر همی بودمی places the adverbials 'here' (ایدر) and 'still/indeed' (همی) before the verb; بودمی is the old first-person past/subjunctive form 'I would be / I would have been', so the clause means 'If not, I would have been here.'
segments.s034-g104: بسی is an intensifier 'much/many' (here qualifying روز). با شما 'with you' marks accompaniment. روز is the plural sense 'days' in context (many days), and پیمودمی is the first-person past 'I traversed/spent' (poetic past form conveying 'I would have spent/travelled many days with you'). Word order places the time-object before the verb for metrical and emphatic reasons.
segments.s034-g65: به تندی is an adverbial phrase meaning “quickly/with haste” placed before the verb for emphasis. ببستش is past tense with the object clitic -ش (“he bound him/it”); the verb appears before its objects (دو دست و میان), so the phrase reads literally “quickly he bound him (by) two hands and the middle.”
segments.s034-g66: که here introduces the purpose/result clause “so that…”; نگشاید is a negative jussive/optative sense (would not/should not open or loosen) used in the classical style. آن بند is “that fetter/bond,” and پیل ژیان is a poetic description (literally “elephant‑(like) life”) meaning something huge/elephantine — together: “that mighty fetter.”
segments.s034-g67: نشست here is a past action meaning “sat/settled” or, in this context with از بر, more idiomatically “rose (from) off” the throne — classical Persian allows such shifts. از بر تخت زرین او is a prepositional phrase ‘from upon his golden throne’ with the possessor او (‘his’) after the noun.
segments.s034-g68: بیفگند is past tense ‘he threw/cast (away)’ (bi‑afkand form) and takes ناخوب آیین او as its object: ناخوب (bad) modifies آیین (custom/practice), and او is the possessive pronoun ‘his.’ The whole clause means he discarded his bad custom/practice.
segments.s034-g69: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past (here functioning as ‘he commanded/said’). کردن is the infinitival/light-verb following it (literally ‘to do’), and به در بر خروش is the locative/goal phrase ‘at the gate, with a cry’ (بر خروش = to raise a cry/uproar). Together they form “he commanded to raise a cry at the gate.”
segments.s034-g70: که introduces the content of the command (“that …”). هر کس که is a relative phrase ‘whoever,’ دارید is 2nd‑person plural present ‘you have/possess’ addressed to attendants, and بیدار هوش is a descriptive nominal phrase ‘awake and alert’ (بیدار adjective + هوش noun meaning awareness). The clause = “whoever you have who is awake and alert.”
segments.s034-g71: نباید is an impersonal/modal negative ‘must not/should not.’ که introduces the subordinate clause; باشید is the (subjunctive/optative) present 2nd‑person plural ‘you be.’ با + ساز جنگ is a prepositional phrase ‘with the means/instruments of war’ (ساز here = device/means rather than musical instrument).
segments.s034-g72: نه negates the following clause (‘nor’). زین گونه is an idiom meaning ‘in this manner/way’ (زین here is archaic demonstrative; the provided sense list does not include that sense, so it is marked separately). جوید is present/future ‘seeks’ or ‘desires,’ کسی = ‘anyone,’ and نام و ننگ = ‘name (fame) and disgrace/shame.’ The clause = ‘nor should anyone in this way seek both fame and shame.’

sentences.s034-s9: Everyone was longing for Fereydun, for they were bloodied by the afflictions of Zahhak.
sentences.s034-s10: From the walls (came) bricks, and from the roofs stones.
sentences.s034-s11: It fell like dew from a black cloud; there was no place on the ground for a foot.
sentences.s034-s12: Into the city’s inner quarters, every young man was (there)—and likewise the elders who were wise in battle.
sentences.s034-s69: He became severed from himself, and his bond remained thus in his fetter.
sentences.s034-s17: Troops and townsfolk, heaped like a mountain, the whole company came into the battle.
sentences.s034-s18: From that bright city a dark cloud of dust arose, so that the sun became deep blue (lapis-colored).
sentences.s034-s19: Then Zahhâk, seeking a remedy, set his face from the host toward the palace.
sentences.s034-s20: He clad his whole body in iron so that no one in the assembly would know him.
sentences.s034-s45: Fereydun the wise appointed them in order and established them on their posts.
sentences.s034-s46: He repeatedly gave them counsel and uttered praise; he likewise recalled the World‑Creator.
sentences.s034-s47: He would say that this seat is mine; by a good/auspicious star your land is bright.
sentences.s034-s48: For the pure God raised us up out of the company from Mount Alborz.
sentences.s034-s41: You have lingered long and are cheerful; go in peace toward your training.
sentences.s034-s42: They all heard straightaway the king’s words about that man’s caution with the court.
sentences.s034-s43: And after that, all the famed men of the city—no one sought evil from the crown nor greed from the treasure for himself.
sentences.s034-s44: They departed with peace and desire; all hearts were made obedient to his command.
sentences.s034-s5: He unjustly took from me the palace's roof and doors, and, in hatred, brought in the head.
sentences.s034-s6: When Fereydun's army became aware, everyone headed toward that road heedlessly.
sentences.s034-s7: They poured down on war-horses and, in that narrow place, sprang upon (their foes).
sentences.s034-s8: On every roof and at every doorway of the townspeople there was someone ready for battle.
sentences.s034-s29: The auspicious messenger Soroush did not come to the hem; "Do not strike," he said, "for his time has not come."
sentences.s034-s30: Bind the broken Hamidun like a stone; carry him until two mountains come close before you.
sentences.s034-s31: His fetter lay within the mountain; no cutting will come upon his nature and his bond.
sentences.s034-s32: When Fereydun heard (this), he did not remain long at rest; he fashioned a lasso from lion-skin.
sentences.s034-s1: The sovereign Zahhak, because of that conversation, boiled over with anger and quickly turned his face (set out).
sentences.s034-s2: When night, the turning of the day, struck the compass, it struck the radiant one's bead into qar.
sentences.s034-s3: He commanded that they take up the saddles here and place them on those fleet‑footed, keen‑eyed ones.
sentences.s034-s4: Daman appeared with a heavy army — all the virile divs, warriors.
sentences.s034-s57: Many a time has passed over mountain and plain, and many more will pass.
sentences.s034-s58: In that manner they bound Zahhak fast and carried the ill-fated one toward the lion-feast.
sentences.s034-s59: They kept driving him into the mountain interior; they intended to bring about his overthrow.
sentences.s034-s60: Then that auspicious Soroush also arrived and whispered a certain secret into his ear.
sentences.s034-s37: A soldier should not, together with a craftsman, pursue both skills in the same way.
sentences.s034-s38: One should be a craftsman and another a mace-bearer; each person's fitting task becomes apparent.
sentences.s034-s39: When this craft seeks that and that seeks this, the whole land becomes tumultuous.
sentences.s034-s40: He who was corrupt is in bonds; the world was fearful because of his deeds.
sentences.s034-s53: The guests kissed the ground before him; from the court rose the sound of the drum.
sentences.s034-s54: Continuously the army issued forth from the city, and from that city having found no one for that purpose...
sentences.s034-s55: They led Zahhak away bound and humiliated; having flung him, wretched, upon the back of a beast.
sentences.s034-s56: He kept driving them off in this manner up to the lion's lair; when you hear this, O aged reciter of the world...
sentences.s034-s21: She took the snare into her inner grasp and climbed up onto the high palace roof.
sentences.s034-s22: He saw that dark-eyed Narges, proud of the city — filled with enchantment, secretly with Fereydun.
sentences.s034-s23: Two cheeks like the day and two tresses like the night; her lip opened to Zahhak's curse.
sentences.s034-s24: A fire of envy rose in her mind; she straightway cast the snare into the iwan.
sentences.s034-s61: Take this parcel up to Mount Damavand, thereby leaving the Arabs without companions.
sentences.s034-s62: Do not take anyone except someone who will not forsake you and who, in the time of hardship, will take you to his arms.
sentences.s034-s63: He brought Zahhak — like (one called) Navand — to Mount Damavand and bound him in fetters.
sentences.s034-s64: He chose a narrow spot inside the mountain, kept watch, and rendered the cave's seat unseen.
sentences.s034-s25: He thought neither of the throne nor of his precious life; he came down from the roof of the lofty palace.
sentences.s034-s26: In his hand was a bluish dagger; he was thirsty for the blood of the fair‑faced ones.
sentences.s034-s27: From above, when he set his foot on the ground, Fereydun came like the wind.
sentences.s034-s28: At that, Gâusar laid his hand on the mace and struck at his head; the rag was broken to pieces.
sentences.s034-s65: He drove heavy nails into a place where his mind (brain) was not.
sentences.s034-s66: He fastened his hand down upon that mountain again, so that it would remain long and hard to remove.
sentences.s034-s67: He bound him so that he hung in that manner, and from him the heart’s blood gushed onto the ground.
sentences.s034-s68: Zahhak’s name was turned to dust, and because of his evil the whole world was cleansed.
sentences.s034-s13: They turned toward Afrīdūn's army; they came out from Zahhāk's trickery.
sentences.s034-s14: A clamour rose from the fire‑temple: 'If there is a king on the throne, let him be an elder.'
sentences.s034-s15: All — old and young — obey his command; we will not pass over one word of his speech.
sentences.s034-s16: We will not go to Zahhāk's court — that dragon‑shouldered, impure one.
sentences.s034-s49: Know that the world will be freed from the dragon's evil at the command of my mace.
sentences.s034-s50: When the benefactor granted forgiveness, one should entrust his release to goodness.
sentences.s034-s51: I am the headman of the whole world; it is not fitting to sit in one place.
sentences.s034-s52: But if not, I would have remained here; I would have spent many days with you.
sentences.s034-s33: He quickly bound his two hands and his midsection so that that great fetter would not come undone.
sentences.s034-s34: He rose from (off) his golden throne and cast away his bad custom.
sentences.s034-s35: He ordered that a shout be raised at the gate: ‘Let anyone you have who is awake and alert…’
sentences.s034-s36: You must not be given to the instruments (means) of war; nor should anyone in this way seek both fame and shame.
